sections.heading: Product Description
sections.content:
Product Name: Bond-Repair Leave-In Cream
Product Form: Leave-in cream (rinse-off free)
Primary Packaging: 150mL HDPE pump bottle
Product Category: Haircare – Repair & Treatment

sections.heading: Batch Scale Summary
sections.content:
The following batch scales have been defined for this product:

• Laboratory Scale: 10 kg
• Pilot Scale: 100 kg
• Commercial Scale: 1,000 kg

Scale-up ratios should be validated at each stage before progression. Equipment selection must be appropriate to each batch size, with particular attention to mixing geometry and heat transfer efficiency as scale increases.

sections.heading: Process Overview
sections.content:
The manufacturing process for the Bond-Repair Leave-In Cream follows a standard emulsion or cream manufacturing workflow with a critical cool-down addition step for heat-sensitive bond-repair actives.

High-level process stages:
1. Raw material dispensing and quality checks
2. Main phase preparation (water and oil phases as applicable)
3. Overhead mixing and homogenisation
4. Cool-down phase
5. Addition of heat-sensitive actives at or below 40°C
6. Final mixing and quality adjustment
7. In-process quality control (IPC) testing
8. Bulk hold prior to filling
9. Filling into 150mL HDPE pump bottles
10. Capping, labelling, and packaging
11. Final QC release

sections.heading: Mixing Requirements
sections.content:
Mixing Method: Standard overhead mixing
Mixer Type: Overhead anchor or paddle mixer [specific equipment model to be confirmed per site]
Homogeniser: [To be confirmed — assess need based on final formula viscosity]

Key mixing parameters:
• Mixing speed: [To be confirmed based on formula development data]
• Mixing duration per stage: [To be confirmed]
• Vessel type: Jacketed stainless steel vessel recommended for temperature control
• Deaeration: [To be confirmed — assess if vacuum mixing is required]

sections.heading: Heat-Sensitive Active Addition Protocol
sections.content:
Critical Process Parameter: Bond-repair actives must be added only when the bulk batch temperature has cooled to 40°C or below.

• Premix or pre-dissolve actives as required prior to addition [method to be confirmed by formulation team]
• Verify batch temperature using calibrated in-line thermometer or thermocouple before addition
• Add actives slowly with continued overhead mixing
• Avoid high-shear mixing after active addition unless validated to be safe
• Record temperature at time of addition in the batch manufacturing record (BMR)

Failure to observe this temperature limit may result in degradation of bond-repair actives and reduced product efficacy.

sections.heading: Annual Volume Planning
sections.content:
Target Annual Volume: 300,000 units by Year 2
Unit Size: 150mL per unit
Total Annual Volume (Year 2): 300,000 × 150mL = 45,000,000mL = 45,000 litres

Assuming 1,000 kg commercial batches and approximate bulk density [to be confirmed]:
• Estimated batches per year: approximately 45–50 commercial batches [to be confirmed once bulk density and fill weight are established]

Production planning should account for:
• Equipment availability and maintenance downtime
• Raw material lead times
• Filling line capacity for 150mL HDPE pump bottles
• Seasonal demand fluctuations [to be confirmed with commercial team]

sections.content: The regulatory information in this document is provided for guidance only. Please verify all regulatory requirements with your qualified regulatory affairs team before taking action.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.content: This document outlines the equipment requirements for the manufacture of Bond-Repair Leave-In Cream at laboratory, pilot, and commercial scales. Equipment must be selected, qualified, and maintained in accordance with site GMP procedures.

sections.heading: Manufacturing Vessels
sections.content:
All manufacturing vessels should be constructed of 316L stainless steel, be CIP/SIP compatible where applicable, and have jacketed walls for temperature control.

Laboratory Scale (10 kg):
• Vessel capacity: 15–20L (to allow headspace for mixing)
• Jacket type: Water-heated/cooled jacket or hot plate with temperature probe
• Equipment model: [To be confirmed]

Pilot Scale (100 kg):
• Vessel capacity: 150–200L
• Jacket type: Jacketed vessel with external circulating water bath
• Equipment model: [To be confirmed]

Commercial Scale (1,000 kg):
• Vessel capacity: 1,200–1,500L
• Jacket type: Full jacketed vessel with dedicated heating/cooling utility supply
• Equipment model: [To be confirmed]

sections.heading: Mixing Equipment
sections.content:
Mixing Type Required: Standard overhead mixing

All scales:
• Mixer type: Overhead anchor, gate, or paddle mixer
• Variable speed drive required: Yes (to allow RPM control at different process stages)
• Direction of mixing: [To be confirmed — typically counter-rotating for creams]
• Shaft and impeller material: 316L stainless steel

Homogeniser (if required):
• To be confirmed by formulation team based on viscosity and emulsion stability data
• Recommended assessment: conduct lab trials with and without in-line homogeniser

Note: High-shear mixing should NOT be used after addition of heat-sensitive bond-repair actives unless validated.

sections.heading: Temperature Monitoring and Control
sections.content:
Temperature control is a critical process requirement for this product due to heat-sensitive active addition at ≤ 40°C.

Required equipment:
• Calibrated in-line thermocouples or RTD probes in each manufacturing vessel
• Continuous temperature logging capability (preferred) or manual logging at defined intervals
• Calibration frequency: [Per site calibration schedule — to be confirmed]
• Alarm set point: Set alert at 42°C to allow operator response before 40°C limit is breached
• External portable thermometer for independent verification: Recommended

sections.heading: Filling Equipment
sections.content:
Pack Format: 150mL HDPE pump bottle

Filling line requirements:
• Filling principle: Volumetric or gravimetric filling [to be confirmed based on bulk viscosity]
• Fill head type: [To be confirmed — compatible with cream viscosity range]
• Pump dispenser assembly: Automated or semi-automated torquing equipment required
• Filling speed target: [To be confirmed based on 300,000 units/year capacity planning]
• Filling line: [To be confirmed — dedicated or shared line]

Capacity Check:
• 300,000 units/year ÷ available production days [to be confirmed] = [units per day to be calculated]
• Filling line must be validated to meet this throughput.

sections.heading: Ancillary Equipment
sections.content:
The following ancillary equipment will also be required:

• Calibrated weighing scales (appropriate to batch size at each scale)
• pH meter with calibrated electrodes (calibrated minimum twice daily during manufacture)
• Viscometer [model and spindle to be confirmed based on formula viscosity range]
• Transfer pumps: [To be confirmed — must be compatible with cream viscosity]
• Bulk holding tanks: [Capacity and number to be confirmed]
• HDPE pump bottle storage: [To be confirmed — appropriate clean storage prior to filling]

sections.heading: Equipment Qualification
sections.content:
All manufacturing equipment must be qualified before use in production:

• IQ (Installation Qualification): Confirm equipment installed per specification
• OQ (Operational Qualification): Confirm equipment operates within defined parameters
• PQ (Performance Qualification): Confirm equipment consistently produces product meeting specifications

Qualification schedule and responsible party: [To be confirmed with site Engineering and QA teams]

sections.heading: Purpose and Scope
sections.content: This document outlines the scale-up pathway for the Bond-Repair Leave-In Cream from laboratory development through to commercial manufacture. It is intended to guide the Manufacturing team in managing the transition across the three defined batch scales and to identify critical process parameters that must be monitored and validated at each stage.

sections.heading: Scale-Up Stages
sections.content:
Stage 1 – Laboratory Scale
• Batch size: 10 kg
• Objective: Establish and lock manufacturing process, confirm critical process parameters (CPPs), generate initial stability and IPC data
• Owner: R&D / Formulation team
• Success criteria: Formula meets specification; CPPs identified and documented

Stage 2 – Pilot Scale
• Batch size: 100 kg
• Objective: Validate scale-up of process; confirm mixing times, temperature profiles, and active addition protocol translate from lab scale; generate pilot stability batches
• Owner: Pilot plant / Manufacturing team in collaboration with R&D
• Success criteria: Pilot batch meets all IPC and release specifications; no significant deviations from lab-scale process

Stage 3 – Commercial Scale
• Batch size: 1,000 kg
• Objective: Full process validation; confirm consistent manufacture at commercial scale ahead of 300,000 units/year production
• Owner: Commercial Manufacturing team
• Success criteria: Minimum [3 consecutive batches — to be confirmed with QA] meet all specifications; process validation report approved

sections.heading: Critical Process Parameters (CPPs)
sections.content:
The following CPPs have been identified and must be monitored and controlled at all scales:

• Temperature at bond-repair active addition: Must be ≤ 40°C — this is the primary thermal critical process parameter
• Mixing speed and duration: Must be defined and maintained consistently across scales
• Order of addition of ingredients: Must follow defined sequence, particularly for heat-sensitive actives
• pH of final bulk: [Target range to be confirmed by formulation team]
• Viscosity of final bulk: [Target range to be confirmed]
• Cool-down rate: [To be confirmed — rapid or controlled cool-down may affect texture and emulsion stability]

sections.heading: Key Scale-Up Considerations
sections.content:
The following factors must be assessed and managed during scale-up:

• Heat Transfer: Larger vessels have a lower surface area-to-volume ratio, meaning cool-down to ≤ 40°C will take significantly longer at 100 kg and 1,000 kg compared to 10 kg. Jacketed vessels with active cooling are essential. Cool-down time studies must be conducted at each scale.

• Mixing Efficiency: Overhead mixer geometry changes at scale. Impeller diameter, tip speed, and mixing patterns must be reviewed. Mixing times may need to be extended at larger scales.

• Active Addition Logistics: At commercial scale (1,000 kg), the volume of bond-repair actives to be added at cool-down will be significantly larger. Pre-weigh and pre-mix procedures for actives must be scaled accordingly.

• Filling Line Integration: Bulk transfer from 1,000 kg manufacturing vessel to filling line must be planned to avoid temperature rise above 40°C during transfer [assess insulated transfer lines or temperature monitoring during transfer].

• In-Process Sampling: Sampling points and frequencies must be adjusted for larger vessel sizes to ensure representative IPC results.

sections.heading: Technology Transfer Documentation Requirements
sections.content:
The following documents must be completed and approved as part of the technology transfer process:

• Master Formula document (from R&D to Manufacturing)
• Manufacturing process description and SOPs
• Batch Manufacturing Record (BMR) templates for each scale
• In-process control (IPC) specifications
• Finished product release specifications
• Analytical methods and validation data
• Stability protocol and data summary (at time of transfer)
• Risk assessment for scale-up [to be completed]
• Equipment qualification records (IQ/OQ/PQ) for all relevant equipment

sections.heading: Timeline
sections.content:
Indicative milestones (dates to be confirmed with project team):

• Laboratory scale manufacturing complete: [To be confirmed]
• Pilot scale trials complete: [To be confirmed]
• Pilot stability data available: [To be confirmed]
• Commercial scale process validation batches complete: [To be confirmed]
• Year 2 commercial production ramp to 300,000 units/year: [To be confirmed]

sections.heading: Purpose and Scope
sections.content: This risk assessment identifies key manufacturing risks associated with the production of Bond-Repair Leave-In Cream at laboratory, pilot, and commercial scale. It is intended to support process development, scale-up, and commercial manufacturing planning. Risks should be reviewed and updated as the project progresses.

sections.heading: Risk 1 – Premature Addition of Heat-Sensitive Actives
sections.content:
Risk Description: Bond-repair actives added to batch before temperature has cooled to ≤ 40°C, resulting in degradation of actives and loss of product efficacy.

Likelihood: Medium — cool-down times will be longer at pilot and commercial scale and may create pressure to add actives earlier.

Impact: High — degraded actives would compromise the core claim and efficacy of the product.

Mitigation measures:
• Mandatory temperature verification step in BMR before active addition
• In-line thermocouple with alarm set at 42°C to alert operator prior to threshold
• Double sign-off (operator + supervisor) on temperature confirmation
• Cool-down time studies conducted at each scale to provide estimated waiting times
• Operator training on criticality of this step

Residual Risk: Low (with controls in place)

sections.heading: Risk 2 – Emulsion Instability During Scale-Up
sections.content:
Risk Description: Changes in mixing geometry, impeller tip speed, and heat transfer at larger scales may result in emulsion instability, phase separation, or texture changes.

Likelihood: Medium — scale-up of emulsion systems frequently requires process adjustment.

Impact: High — unstable bulk cannot be filled and would result in batch failure and rework/loss.

Mitigation measures:
• Conduct structured scale-up trials with defined IPC checks at each scale
• Review mixing tip speed equivalence (not just RPM) when moving between scales
• Conduct accelerated stability testing on pilot batches before proceeding to commercial scale
• Engage formulation team in pilot scale trials

Residual Risk: Medium (to be reassessed after pilot trials)

sections.heading: Risk 3 – Insufficient Production Capacity for Year 2 Volume Target
sections.content:
Risk Description: Manufacturing site may not have sufficient filling line capacity or batch scheduling availability to produce 300,000 units per year by Year 2.

Likelihood: Medium — dependent on site capacity planning and lead time for equipment.

Impact: High — failure to meet commercial supply commitments.

Mitigation measures:
• Conduct capacity planning analysis using 1,000 kg batch size and 150mL fill data [to be completed]
• Confirm filling line throughput rate and available production days per year
• Identify and address any bottlenecks (mixing, filling, packaging) early in the scale-up process
• Consider dual-source or contract manufacturer as contingency [to be confirmed with commercial team]

Residual Risk: [To be confirmed after capacity analysis]

sections.heading: Risk 4 – Raw Material Supply or Quality Failure for Bond-Repair Actives
sections.content:
Risk Description: Bond-repair actives may have limited supplier availability, long lead times, or quality variability that could impact manufacturing continuity.

Likelihood: [To be confirmed — dependent on active ingredient supply chain]

Impact: High — the bond-repair actives are the core functional ingredient of the product.

Mitigation measures:
• Identify primary and secondary suppliers for bond-repair actives [to be confirmed]
• Establish incoming QC testing for actives (identity, purity, potency where applicable)
• Define minimum stock levels and reorder points
• Review storage conditions for actives (temperature, humidity, light sensitivity) and ensure site capability

Residual Risk: [To be confirmed]

sections.heading: Risk 5 – Pump Compatibility with Formula Viscosity
sections.content:
Risk Description: The selected pump dispenser may not function correctly if the final formula viscosity is outside the pump's operating range, resulting in dispensing failures or consumer complaints.

Likelihood: Medium — leave-in creams can vary significantly in viscosity.

Impact: Medium — functional packaging failure affecting consumer experience and brand reputation.

Mitigation measures:
• Confirm final formula viscosity target with formulation team
• Test pump performance across the expected viscosity range (including min and max tolerance)
• Conduct pump compatibility testing as part of packaging qualification
• Include pump function as an IPC check during filling

Residual Risk: Low (with testing completed)

sections.heading: Risk Summary Table
sections.content:
Risk 1 – Premature active addition: Likelihood Medium | Impact High | Residual Risk Low
Risk 2 – Emulsion instability at scale: Likelihood Medium | Impact High | Residual Risk Medium
Risk 3 – Insufficient production capacity: Likelihood Medium | Impact High | Residual Risk TBC
Risk 4 – Active ingredient supply failure: Likelihood TBC | Impact High | Residual Risk TBC
Risk 5 – Pump viscosity incompatibility: Likelihood Medium | Impact Medium | Residual Risk Low

All risks should be reviewed and updated following pilot scale trials and prior to commercial validation.
